name: Newspapers and magazines
summary: Usually recyclable when the paper is clean and dry.
steps: Keep the paper dry and free from food contamination.
Bundle or stack it neatly if needed.
Place it in a paper recycling point or a community collection channel.
note: Wet, greasy, or heavily soiled paper should not go with clean paper recycling.

name: Cardboard box
summary: Recyclable when flattened and kept reasonably clean.
steps: Empty the box completely.
Flatten it to save space.
Remove excess tape, foam, or plastic wrapping when practical.
note: Greasy cardboard, such as heavily soiled food boxes, should not go into clean paper recycling.

name: Office paper and textbooks
summary: Generally recyclable if the pages are dry and mostly clean.
steps: Keep the paper dry.
Remove plastic covers, binders, or large metal accessories if possible.
Place the paper in a paper collection point.
note: Mixed materials should be separated where practical.

name: Receipts and thermal paper
summary: These are usually not treated the same way as ordinary recyclable paper.
steps: Keep them out of clean paper recycling.
Dispose of them with ordinary refuse.
note: Small coated or thermal paper slips are not a good fit for regular paper recycling streams.

name: Used tissue paper and paper towels
summary: Usually not accepted in clean paper recycling.
steps: Wrap or bag them if needed.
Dispose of them with general waste.
note: Once tissue products are used or contaminated, they should stay out of paper recycling.

name: Plastic beverage bottle
summary: Commonly recyclable when emptied and lightly rinsed.
steps: Empty the bottle completely.
Rinse it quickly if there is leftover liquid.
Remove caps and labels when practical, then place it in plastics recycling.
note: Cleaner and drier bottles are easier to recycle.

name: Plastic shampoo or detergent bottle
summary: Usually recyclable if the bottle is empty and reasonably clean.
steps: Use up the contents first.
Rinse out heavy residue if possible.
Place the bottle in a plastics recycling stream.
note: Heavily contaminated containers should be cleaned first; otherwise they may be rejected.

name: Clean takeaway plastic container
summary: Recyclable only if food residue is removed and the container is reasonably clean.
steps: Empty all leftover food.
Rinse the container if needed.
Separate mixed materials such as paper sleeves or metal cutlery before recycling.
note: If the container is greasy or badly contaminated and cannot be cleaned, treat it as general waste.

name: Plastic bag
summary: Clean plastic bags can usually go into plastics collection channels.
steps: Shake out food scraps or rubbish first.
Keep the bag reasonably clean and dry.
Put it into a plastics collection point.
note: Dirty bags with food residue should not be mixed with clean plastics.

name: Snack packet or instant noodle pouch
summary: Composite packaging is usually not accepted in standard plastics recycling.
steps: Empty the packet fully.
Dispose of it with general waste unless a specialised collection route is available.
note: Multi-layer packaging is much harder to recycle than simple plastic containers.

name: Aluminium can
summary: A common recyclable metal item once emptied and rinsed.
steps: Empty the can completely.
Rinse out remaining drink.
Place it in a metals recycling point.
note: Crushing the can is optional, but keeping it clean helps.

name: Tin can or metal food can
summary: Usually recyclable after it has been emptied and cleaned.
steps: Empty all food residue.
Rinse the can if needed.
Place it into a metals recycling point.
note: Sharp edges should be handled carefully.

name: Glass beverage bottle
summary: Usually recyclable through glass bottle collection channels.
steps: Empty and rinse the bottle.
Remove caps, pumps, or non-glass parts when practical.
Place the bottle in a glass bottle collection point.
note: This guidance is for container glass such as beverage bottles, not for mirrors or ceramics.

name: Glass food jar or sauce bottle
summary: Usually recyclable when the container glass is empty and clean.
steps: Empty the contents fully.
Rinse out sauces or food residue.
Remove lids when practical and place the glass in collection.
note: Ceramics, Pyrex, and mirrors should not be mixed with container-glass recycling.

name: Mirror or broken ceramic mug
summary: These are usually not accepted in ordinary glass bottle recycling.
steps: Wrap sharp pieces safely before disposal.
Keep them out of bottle-glass recycling.
note: Bottle-glass programmes are meant for container glass, not ceramics or mirrors.

name: Beverage carton
summary: Can be recycled through dedicated carton collection routes when cleaned and flattened.
steps: Empty the carton fully.
Rinse it, drain it, and flatten it.
Remove straws or extra wrapping when practical, then use a carton recycling channel.
note: Cartons need a dedicated collection route and should not be treated exactly like ordinary paper.

name: Rechargeable battery or power bank
summary: Use a dedicated rechargeable battery or special recycling collection route.
steps: Do not place it in ordinary recycling bins.
Tape exposed terminals when practical.
Bring it to a suitable battery or community collection point.
note: Damaged, swollen, or leaking batteries should be handled with extra care.

name: Disposable household battery
summary: These are not usually handled the same way as rechargeable batteries.
steps: Keep them separate from rechargeable batteries.
Follow ordinary household disposal arrangements unless a dedicated local programme is available.
note: Do not assume every battery collection point accepts disposable household batteries.

name: Fluorescent tube or compact fluorescent lamp
summary: Use a dedicated lamp collection route instead of regular bins.
steps: Keep the lamp unbroken if possible.
Return it through a suitable collection channel for fluorescent lamps.
Avoid mixing it with ordinary glass or general waste unless no special route is available.
note: These lamps need more careful handling than ordinary household waste.

name: Small electrical appliance
summary: Use a designated collection channel for recyclable small electrical appliances.
steps: Unplug the appliance and keep it dry.
Bundle loose wires if practical.
Take it to a suitable community collection channel for small electrical appliances.
note: Acceptance can vary by item type, especially for specialised medical or personal-care devices.

name: Large electrical appliance or regulated e-waste
summary: Use seller take-back or an approved collection service instead of ordinary disposal.
steps: Do not leave the item in ordinary recycling bins.
Arrange seller removal or an approved collection service where available.
Follow the relevant e-waste collection route for large or regulated equipment.
note: Large or regulated electrical equipment usually needs a dedicated removal arrangement.

name: Food scraps and fruit peels
summary: Keep food scraps separate from dry recyclables and use food-waste collection if available.
steps: Drain excess liquid first when possible.
Use a food-waste collection point if your building or estate provides one.
Otherwise follow your building’s ordinary refuse arrangement.
note: Food waste collection availability can vary by building and district.
